race.name: Bowral Classic
race.tagline: Australia's premier cycling gran fondo through the rolling hills and heritage towns of the Southern Highlands -- 150 km, 2,393 m climbing, spring in Don Bradman's country.

vitals.registration: Online via bowralclassic.com.au or cyclingclassics.com.au. Cost: AUD $165-315 depending on distance and registration tier.
vitals.entry_fee: AUD $165-315 (varies by distance and registration tier)
vitals.feed_zones: Fully resourced rest stops along all courses with water, electrolytes, snacks, and mechanical support
vitals.cutoff_time: Course-specific cut-off times enforced at rest stops
route_options: 150km Maxi Classic: 152.1 km (94 mi), 2,393 m climbing, 15.2% max gradient, 3 K/QOM segments
120km Challenge Classic: 122.9 km (76 mi), 2,029 m climbing, 15.2% max gradient, 3 K/QOM segments
85km Rouleurs Classic: 85.7 km (53 mi), 1,324 m climbing, 11% max gradient, 1 K/QOM segment

climate.description: Mid-October in the Southern Highlands brings pleasant spring cycling conditions. Bowral sits at 690 m elevation, creating a cooler microclimate than coastal Sydney. Expect daytime temperatures of 15-22C (59-72F) with cool mornings around 8-12C (46-54F). Rainfall averages 54-80 mm for October -- scattered showers are possible but prolonged rain is uncommon. The region is known for four distinct seasons, and October delivers the best of spring: clear skies, moderate temperatures, and green rolling countryside.
challenges: Morning chill at 690 m elevation -- arm warmers needed for early start
Spring rain showers possible -- descents can become slick on painted road markings
Occasional strong westerly winds across exposed highland ridges
UV exposure is significant in Australian spring even at moderate temperatures

terrain.primary: Paved rolling hills through Southern Highlands countryside
terrain.surface: 100% sealed roads through the Southern Highlands region. Mix of B-roads and rural lanes connecting historic towns. Generally well-maintained country roads with some rough patches on quieter sections. All courses start from Bong Bong Street, Bowral and finish at Loseby Park, Bowral.
features: Mt Gibraltar -- the final K/QOM climb for Maxi and Challenge riders, returning through Range Road to the finish
Robertson climbs -- undulating terrain through the rainforest-edge town at the southern end of the course
Kangaloon and Glenquarry rollers -- relentless short climbs through dairy country
Maximum gradient 15.2% on the steepest pitches
3 K/QOM segments on 150km and 120km courses, 1 on 85km course
Rolling terrain throughout -- very few flat sections, constant up and down

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 57. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 56.

course_description.character: A rolling pastoral odyssey through the Southern Highlands of New South Wales. The course is characterised by relentless undulation rather than sustained alpine climbing -- the road is almost never flat, constantly rising and falling through green hills, dairy country, and heritage villages. The 150km Maxi Classic passes through Bowral, Berrima, Moss Vale, Sutton Forest, Bundanoon, Robertson, Kangaloon, and Glenquarry before the final assault on Mt Gibraltar and the finish at Loseby Park. The terrain rewards steady tempo riding and punishes early heroics. Three K/QOM segments provide competitive structure within the rolling landscape.
course_description.signature_challenge: The relentless undulation. There is no single monster climb -- the Southern Highlands course kills you with a thousand cuts. Constant rolling terrain across 150km means your legs never fully recover between efforts. Mt Gibraltar at the finish is the exclamation point, but the real challenge is managing fatigue through 140km of terrain that never lets you rest.
suffering_zones: The middle section of the Maxi Classic takes riders south through Bundanoon and into Robertson via the undulating terrain at the southern edge of the Southern Highlands. The gradients steepen here, the road becomes more isolated, and the accumulated kilometres start to tell. Robertson sits near the escarpment edge with views east toward the Illawarra coast. The climbs out of Robertson heading back north through Kangaloon are where the day's intentions are revised downward.

The approach to the Glenquarry K/QOM is a grinding succession of short climbs through dairy farming country. Each individual rise is manageable, but the relentless up-and-down on tired legs creates a cumulative fatigue that saps the spirit. There is nowhere to hide on these exposed highland roads. The Glenquarry K/QOM segment tests climbing legs that have already done 70+ km of rolling terrain.

The course returns to Bowral via Range Road and the climb of Mt Gibraltar -- a short, sharp ascent to the summit at 863m. After 140+ km of rolling terrain, the gradient hits hard. This is the final K/QOM for the Maxi and Challenge courses, and the last significant effort before the descent to the Loseby Park finish. The climb is steep enough to separate riders who paced intelligently from those who went too deep too early.

history.origin_story: The Bowral Classic was born in 2016 when Sydney-based Yaffa Media, publisher of Australia's leading cycling magazine Bicycling Australia, decided to create a cycling gran fondo worthy of the Southern Highlands region. James Yaffa devised a media and marketing campaign that generated $3 million in exposure from a $50,000 investment. An initial target of 1,000 riders was obliterated when more than 3,000 cyclists from across Australia and eight overseas countries descended on Bowral on 23 October 2016 for the inaugural event. Participants raised $109,000 for charity in that first edition alone. The event was centred on Bowral -- the largest town in the Southern Highlands, famous as the birthplace of cricket legend Sir Donald Bradman -- and the surrounding countryside that had long been a favourite of Sydney cyclists for its rolling terrain and picturesque villages. The success of the Bowral Classic spawned the Cycling Classics series, including the Clare Classic (South Australia) and Noosa Classic (Queensland). By 2024, the events had collectively raised over $500,000 for Australian charities.
notable_moments: 2016: Inaugural event. 3,000+ riders from across Australia and 8 countries. $109,000 raised for charity. Immediate success.
2017: Wins 'Best New Event' and 'Best Community Event' at the Australian Event Awards. Establishes credibility beyond cycling.
2018: Cycling Classics brand expands with Clare Classic (SA) and Noosa Classic (QLD) sister events.
2019-2021: Navigates COVID disruptions. Event adapts and continues while other Australian cycling events fold.
2024: Ninth edition. Close to 3,000 riders. Maxi Classic distance nearly sold out. Over 2,500 riders on course.
2025: Tenth anniversary edition on 19 October. Hosted in Extreme Fire Danger conditions (event proceeded). Continues to attract international entries.
history.reputation: Australia's most consistently delivered cycling gran fondo. While flashier events (L'Etape Australia, Cadel Evans) carry bigger names, the Bowral Classic has built its reputation on reliability, community, and a course that genuinely showcases one of Australia's most beautiful regions. Within the Australian cycling community, it is the accessible bucket-list event -- challenging enough to require training, scenic enough to justify the effort, and social enough to bring friends.

biased_opinion.verdict: Southern Highlands Charm, Club Cyclist's Paradise
biased_opinion.summary: The Bowral Classic is what happens when a cycling magazine publisher decides to build their dream gran fondo. Yaffa Media (Bicycling Australia) created this event in 2016 and nailed the formula: scenic rolling terrain through heritage towns, professional organization, an event village with actual good food, and October spring weather that makes riding a pleasure rather than a survival exercise. At 150km with 2,393m of climbing, the Maxi Classic is a genuine challenge without being a suffer-fest. The Southern Highlands -- Berrima, Robertson, Bundanoon, Kangaloon -- provide the kind of pastoral scenery that makes you forget you are working hard. Mt Gibraltar as the final climb before the finish is well-placed to reward the prepared and punish the ambitious. The charity fundraising ($500K+ since 2016) and community support from Wingecarribee Shire give the event a warmth that purely commercial gran fondos lack. What it is not: an international destination event, a competitive race, or a mountain epic. It is a beautifully executed domestic sportive in one of Australia's most charming regions.
strengths: October spring weather in the Southern Highlands -- 15-22C, near-ideal cycling conditions
Professional organization by Yaffa Media with strong Bicycling Australia media support
Event Village at Loseby Park with gourmet food, bars, cycling exhibitors, and live music
Three distance options (85/120/150km) accommodate all fitness levels
Scenic course through historic Southern Highlands towns: Berrima, Robertson, Bundanoon
Strong community support from Wingecarribee Shire Council and local businesses
Charity fundraising program has raised over $500,000 since 2016
Highlanders wave provides competitive structure for strong riders
weaknesses: No international prestige -- unknown outside Australian cycling
Not a race: timing is for personal reference only, no competitive categories or prizes
Expensive for a domestic sportive: $255-315 AUD for the Maxi Classic
Bowral is 90 minutes from Sydney with no practical public transport option for cyclists
Field depth is shallow -- recreational and club riders, no elite presence
Climbing is relentless rolling rather than dramatic alpine passes
All sealed roads with zero technical terrain -- pure fitness test
biased_opinion.bottom_line: The Bowral Classic is the best domestic gran fondo experience in New South Wales -- maybe in Australia outside the Victorian alpine events. The combination of charming Southern Highlands scenery, pleasant spring weather, professional organization, and genuine community makes it a satisfying day on the bike. It will not appear on any international bucket lists, and competitive riders will find the non-race format underwhelming. But if you live in Sydney, ride a road bike, and want one big autumn-spring event on your calendar, this is the one. The fact that it has run successfully every year since 2016 (including through COVID disruptions) while higher-profile events like L'Etape Australia were being cancelled repeatedly says something about the organisers' competence.

final_verdict.one_liner: NSW's premier cycling gran fondo -- Southern Highlands charm, rolling hills, spring weather, and a finish-line festival worth riding for.
final_verdict.should_you_race: If you are based in Sydney or eastern Australia and want a well-organized, scenic gran fondo without the logistical complexity of traveling to the Victorian Alps or the cancellation risk of L'Etape Australia, the Bowral Classic delivers. The 150km Maxi Classic with 2,393m of climbing is a genuine fitness challenge, the Southern Highlands scenery rewards you for every pedal stroke, and the Event Village at the finish is a proper celebration. Spring weather in October makes the riding enjoyable rather than a battle against heat or cold. Skip if you need international prestige, competitive depth, or mountain-scale climbing. Skip if you are flying internationally specifically for this event -- there are better-value destinations for that level of travel investment.
final_verdict.alternatives: For more climbing in Australia: Peaks Challenge Falls Creek (Victoria, 235km, 4,000m). For the Tour de France experience in Australia: L'Etape Australia (NSW, 143km, closed roads). For the pro race connection: Cadel Evans Great Ocean Road Ride (Victoria, WorldTour weekend). For a similar format in a different Australian setting: Clare Classic (South Australia, same Cycling Classics organiser).

videos.title: Australian Gran Fondos Are Hard (150km 2400m) | BOWRAL CLASSIC 2025
videos.description:
This past weekend I did one of Sydney's biggest cycling events, the Bowral Classic. This mammoth event is challenging; with a 150km profile and 2400m elevation. I went down to the Southern Highlands with plenty of company, with over 85 riders from my club signing up for the event. We had an awesome weekend tackling the climbs and enjoying the beautiful landscape. So come along with me in this video to see what it was all about!

Intro 00:00
Driving Down 01:02
Ride Start 02:58
Flying in the Highlands 04:16
Eating up the Kilometers 07:11
Rest Stop 09:41
Final 50km 11:31
Mt Gibraltra 15:38
Finale 17:30

Photos of bikes; pastries and good times ☕🥐📸
Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/josh.kwan/

My rides, routes and activities 🚴🏻🏔🏃🏻
Strava: https://www.strava.com/athletes/31132875 

Like what I do and want to support me? You can buy me a coffee 🙏🏻
https://buymeacoffee.com/joshuakwanu

🍿 23946 Subscribers

videos.curation_reason: High-quality first-person race recap with excellent production value and strong viewership from recent event.

videos.title: Don't Do This on Your Bowral Classic 2025 Grand Fondo
videos.description:
Welcome to my First ever Grand Fondo Cycling experience — the Bowral Classic 2025! 
🚴‍♂️ 150km, 2400vm of pain, laughter, and pure chaos. From getting dropped at the start line to getting lost and missing the final climb, this ride had everything: highs, lows, new friends, and unforgettable moments across the stunning Southern Highlands.

Expect real racing footage, raw emotions, plenty of laughs, and some honest reflections on what it’s like to tackle your first Gran Fondo in Australia.

If you love cycling vlogs, bike banter or just watching someone suffer beautifully on a bike, this one’s for you.

👉 Don’t forget to like, comment, and subscribe for more Aussie cycling adventures!

------------- C - H - A - P - T - E - R - S ------------
00:00 – Intro: My First Ever Bike Race at the Bowral Classic 2025 🚴‍♂️
01:16 – Start Line Nerves: Bowral Classic Begins!
02:50 – Murillo Parking Chaos & Pre-Race Laughs
03:22 – Riding Through the Beautiful Southern Highlands 🌄
04:00 – Finn + Epic Country Views on Course
04:28 – Oxley Climb #1 – The Pain Starts Early 😅

15:51 – THE WALL – Brutal Final Climb Challenge ⛰️

18:40 – Finish Line Relief – Lessons Learned at Bowral Classic
19:38 – Pastries Cycling Club Highlights & Results 🏁
20:25 – CHEERS! Post-Race Celebration & Recovery Beer 🍻

videos.curation_reason: First-timer perspective with raw emotions and authentic experience showing the challenges newcomers face.

videos.title: Getting Gran Fondo Ready! - Bowral Classic 150km
videos.description:
The Bowral Classic Gran Fondo is less than 12 weeks away. This is my B race for the year so I am keen to give it my best shot. Can I finish in the Top 100? What are the most critical considerations needed to get it done?

Learn more about the event https://cyclingclassics.com.au/bowral/

🚴‍♂️Another time crushed local loop. MCR, Akuna Bay East, and West Head 62km 1000m elevation. https://strava.app.link/CKKdj4xjcMb

🏷️ #Cycling #RoadCycling #GranFondo
🎵 Find all the tracks from all my videos in this handy playlist (Referral link) https://share.epidemicsound.com/8lgfzh/?playlist=xlf2mzt3lywqdnhq9dxdo5lbgejd9v11

🎥 Want to try the new Insta360 X4? Support the channel by buying with this link https://www.insta360.com/sal/x4?utm_term=INR0VZA

➡️ https://www.instagram.com/moderatecyclist/
➡️ https://www.strava.com/athletes/bkpaton

00:00 - Introduction
01:30 - Pacing
03:30 - Setup
04:18 - Nutrition
05:38 - Training
07:07 - Wrap-up

the race starting in a later wave
doesn't necessarily affect your time but

deep dish

possible which means ideally no saddle

store a
camera but when you're as tall as me

need to not really
stop so this means I'm going to have to

the race I think I want to hit around

videos.title: HOW TO RIDE A FONDO
videos.description:
Episode 171 || SUBSCRIBE: http://bit.ly/2gMWc1t

So this was sort of sparked by a chat I had with some of the guys I was at the bucks party with over the weekend. They had heard all about l'etape and were wishing me good luck for it. I would then spend the next 10min explaining it wasn't a race ... even though it was timed.

This got me thinking about what the differences between the events are, but more importantly how that actually plays out on the road.

After l'etape I'm keen to compare the ride to some of the races we have done throughout the year as well, to give another perspective on the differences.

Anyway, I hope this doesn't come across to preachy and you guys get something (if not just a laugh) out of it.

##NOTE TO SELF: Don't attempt to VLOG when Jesse tells you he is doing TT efforts##

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------
INSTAGRAM: @chrismiller27 @neroracing
TWITTER: @chrismiller @neroracing
WEB: neroracingbike.com
STRAVA: https://www.strava.com/athletes/186450
BIKE Noah SL - ridley-bikes.com.au
SERVICE & SUPPORT: Cycling Projects Racing Ashbury 
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
All shot on a Sony a6500, Shimano Sports Camera, iPhone 8 Plus, Edited in Final Cut Pro X.

videos.title: I CRASHED Out of the Elite Wave At Bowral Classic...DISASTER
videos.description:
Follow me as I take on the Maxi Classic at the Bowral Classic, a brutal 150km Gran Fondo with roughly 2500 m of climbing through the stunning countryside south of Sydney.

After months of hard training, I earned a spot in the Highlanders Elite Wave (the front pack) and was ready to see how the legs fared against the three main climbs: Oxley Hill, Glenquarry, and Gibraltar.

Out of the gates, the pace was incredible! With closed roads and a lead-out car, we were averaging a just over 37kph across the first 30km. The day couldn't have been more brilliant... until it was over.

At 32km, an unavoidable incident forced me off the bike. 

In an instant, the race ended. My new kit was ruined, my shoes were scuffed, and my ass met the tarmac, leaving me with a few painful souvenirs and a bruised ego. Thankfully, there were no broken bones and the bike survived.

This video is a full, honest breakdown of the event: the high-speed start, the great race organisation, the unexpected disaster, and the reality that crashes are just part of the sport when you're racing at the limit.

Hope you enjoy the ride (and my grazed arse!). Safe to say, I'm already back on the bike. Happy viewing!

[Subscribe for more race vlogs and training videos!]

Strava Link | Bowral Classic 2025 | https://strava.app.link/E9cEq5fmPXb

Socials:
Instagram | https://www.instagram.com/itsmaxmann/
Strava | https://www.strava.com/athletes/56313550

Supporting the channel:
MAAP | https://maap.cc/
Alba Optics | https://albaoptics.cc/
CYCPLUS | https://www.cycplus.com/?ref=ItsMaxMann
Pillar Performance (15% DC: MAX) | https://pillarperformance.shop/

#roadcycling #sydney #cyclist #roadcyclingforbeginners #roadcyclingvlog #roadcyclingtechnique #roadcyclingandweightloss #roadcyclingandbodybuilding #bikerace #bikeracing #bike #bikelover #bikelover #bikeexploration #cyclinglife #roadcyclingtips #roadcyclingmotivation #cyclingshots #cyclingvlog #bikesounds #cyclingnews #longrides #sunshinecoast #queensland #maap #brisbane #racing #crash #crashing
videos.transcript:
crashing on a road bike. We see it all
the time. The Tour to France, the Jurro,
Stratabi in countless other events. It's
not just amateurs hitting the deck. Even
legends like Tad Vaga and Yonas Finger
are coming off their bike. At the
highest level, it's almost a given. It's
just a part of the sport. But crashing
sure isn't fun. To put it into
perspective, imagine jumping out of a
car at 50 km an hour while wearing
practically nothing. It's going to hurt
and you're going to find yourself with a
few tarmac tattoos to show for it. Count
yourself lucky if you walk away without
any broken bones. My first major crash
led to a broken elbow, a few months off
the bike, and a very, very painful
recovery. My second crash, well, that's
what today's video is all about. You're
going to find out how bad it was and
just how good the event was leading up
to it. [music]
[music]
All

videos.curation_reason: Recent elite wave perspective with detailed course information and dramatic storyline that captures the challenging nature of the event.

quotes.text: It was 11° according to the town clock, and people are shivering at the start line. I was incredibly happy to get moving because I was freezing, but I find that once I start moving, I warm up really quickly.
The first climb is Oxley Hill road climb, which is 1.3 km in length and an average gradient of 10.5% - it's only 40km into the ride out of 150km, and we've got two more big ones at the end.
I'm suffering, but it's beautiful country roads here in the Southern Highlands. Can't complain - the beautiful scenery makes me feel a bit better.

key_challenges.name: Oxley Hill
key_challenges.description: First main climb - 1.3 km in length with 10.5% average gradient. Described as 'a little pinch and it is going to hurt.'

key_challenges.name: Glen Quarry
key_challenges.description: Second climb - 1.9 km in length with 5.2% average gradient. Called 'probably one of the easy ones out of the bunch.'

key_challenges.name: Mount Gibraltar
key_challenges.description: The biggest climb of the day - 3.9 km with 5% average gradient. Positioned 'painfully just before the finish line' at around 142 km.

terrain_notes.text: Course is 'quite lumpy throughout and undulating especially for the first 36 km' with plenty of hills and rollers along the way beyond just the three main climbs.

gear_mentions.text: Do not change tires the day or two before - ride new tires for a couple of weeks beforehand to embed them in nicely and get familiar with their grip. Don't run latex tubes.

race_day_tips.text: Try not to stop - stopping is absolute dead time. Carry as much food and water as you can, maybe take extra plastic water bottles to throw in feed zones to keep moving.

Strategy is to hit it hard and fast for the first 36 km as advised by coach - if you can stay with front pack for first 36k, the next 60k is pretty much flat rolling hills where you can sit in wheels.
Plan to wait for teammates at the top of KOM sections but minimize stopping time at rest stops except for the one at 100 km. Goal is to keep momentum going.
Make every move predictable when riding in the big group early on since you don't know the abilities or awareness of other riders. Feel free to go to the front and ride hard on early lumps and bumps.

additional_quotes.text: When my alarm went off at 3:40 a.m., I was kind of regretting my decision and wish I stayed the night before. Barrel is an hour and a half drive from Sydney.

It was 11 degrees according to the town clock at the start line and people are shivering. I was incredibly happy to get moving because I was freezing, but I find that once I start moving, I warm up really quickly and usually need to take my layer off within half an hour.

This year, my club Pastry CC had enormous representation at the event with 85 signups for our team. I think it just goes to show that people love to do these weekends away or these big rides which are a bit more of a challenge.

rider_intel.search_text: The Bowral Classic is a challenging 150km grand fondo in New South Wales featuring 2,400m of elevation gain. Riders describe three main climbs: Oxley Hill at 36km (1.3km at 10.5% gradient), Glen Quarry at 130km (1.9km at 5.2%), and the final Mount Gibraltar at 142km (3.9km at 5%). The course is described as quite lumpy and undulating, especially for the first 36km beyond just the main climbs. Conditions can be very cold at the 6:15am start with temperatures around 9-11 degrees. The race attracts large participation with mass start waves including an elite Highlanders group. Strategy involves hitting the first 36km hard to stay with the front group, then sitting in on the flatter middle section. Key advice includes minimizing stops, carrying extra nutrition, and making predictable moves in large groups early on.
